Determination of cement hydration and pozzolanic reaction extents for ﬂy-ash cement pastes Qiang Zeng a,b, Kefei Li a,⇑, Teddy Fen-chong b, Patrick Dangla b a Civil Engineering Department, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China b Université Paris-Est, Institute Navier, UMR CNRS–LCPC–ENPC, Champs sur Marne 77420, France article info Article history: Received 15 January 2011 Received in revised form 1 June 2011 Accepted 13 July 2011 Available online 19 August 2011 Keywords: Cement paste Fly-ash Hydration extent Non-evaporable water Pozzolanic reaction abstract This article intends to quantify the reaction extents of cement grains and ﬂy-ash (FA) particles in blended cement pastes during their hardening process. To this aim, a synthetic model for cement hydration extent in blended pastes is established taking into account the dilution effect, local w/c augmentation effect as well as heterogeneous nucleation effect of FA particles. Then the cement hydration and FA reaction extents of blended cement pastes with two w/b ratios (0.3, 0.5) and four FA contents (0%, 20%, 40%, 60%) are investigated. The non evaporable water content (Wn) and calcium hydroxide content (CH) were measured by thermal gravity analysis (TGA) and the FA reaction extent was quantiﬁed by the selective dissolution method. On the basis of these experimental data, the cement hydration extent and FA pozzo- lanic reaction extent are determined from both experimental approach and model-based approach. From the results, it is observed that local w/c, heterogeneous nucleation effects and FA hydration have compa- rable contribution to the total Wn content in blended pastes while the FA hydration dominates in CH con- tent. Furthermore, the good agreement between the experimental hydration extents and model-based extents validates the established synthetic model for cement hydration extent in blended pastes. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Fly ash (FA), a by-product of thermal power industry, has been widely used as a supplementary cementitious material (SCM) in con- crete technology for its contribution to the fresh concrete workabil- ity, microstructure formation as well as long term mechanical and durability properties. The dosage of FA in binder materials is usually adapted to different engineering applications and different curing conditions. For instance, for prestressed concrete and reinforcement concrete with early exposure to environment, the ﬂy-ash content, mass ratio of ﬂy-ash in binder, is limited to 25% and 30% according to practice regulations [1]. For underground environment the FA content can be as high as 50% and this value can reach 70% for roller compacted concrete (RCC), used for dam engineering [2]. Fly-ash is a pozzolana additive, not capable to react with water without an alkaline activator. The role of ﬂy-ash during concrete hardening process is relatively well understood nowadays. Firstly, the spherical geometry of ﬂy-ash particles, acting as lubricating agent, helps to improve the rheology properties of fresh paste before its setting. Secondly, the hydration of ﬂy-ash, named as pozzolanic reaction, consumes the cement hydrates (portlandite) and ﬁlls the already-formed pore structure by its own hydration product, thus helping to achieve a more compact pore structure and higher strength for cement-based materials. Fly-ash is believed to have pozzolanic reaction after the age of 3d as blended with cement [3]. This pozzonlanic

Among these methods, TGA can provide reliable measurement on the amount of non-evaporable water (Wn) and the content of calcium hydroxide (CH) in blended cement pastes. According to the hydration mechanisms of cement grains and FA particles, both reactions contribute to Wn but FA reaction consumes CH from cement hydration. With the chemical equations of cement hydra- tion and FA reaction the respective reaction extent for cement and FA can be determined. Following this approach, this article investigates the cement hydration and FA pozzolanic reaction (hydration) extents on the basis of TGA results on Wn and CH. To help the results interpreta- tion, a synthetic model for FA effect on cement hydration extent is ﬁrstly proposed in Section 2. Then materials and experimental details are given in Section 3. Afterwards, the hydration extents of cement grains and FA particles in blended pastes are determined in Section 4 from both experimental and model-based approaches, and the concluding remarks are given in the end. 2. Synthetic model for cement hydration extent 2.1. Effect of w/c ratio As a known fact, large w/c ratio creates in cement pastes a pore structure with high porosity, high connectivity and low strength. However, the cement hydration extent (degree) is more advanced due to more water availability [19]. Classic regression model links the cement hydration extent ac with w/c ratio through the following relation [13], ac ¼ a1 exp /C0 b w=c /C18/C19 ð1Þ where ac,1 are the cement hydration extent and ultimate hydration extent, the constant b characterizes the sensitivity of hydration ex- tent with respect to w/c ratio. In Fig. 1 are summarized the available results in the literature on the ac /C0 w/c relation, with the regressed constants a1, b given in Table 1 . For blended cement pastes incorporating inert ﬁllers or SCM with mass fraction of fp, the w/c ratio can be converted from w/b ratio through, w=c ¼ w=b 1 1 /C0 fp ð2Þ Obviously, under a same w/b ratio the blended cement pastes (fp > 0), compared to the neat cement paste ( fp = 0), have larger cement hydration extent. This hydration extent augmentation, purely due to the ‘‘dilution effect’’ of additives, can be expressed as, Daw=c ¼ ac /C0 a0 ð3Þ where a0 is the hydration extent of neat cement paste. Combining Eqs. (1) and (3) gives, Daw=c a0 ¼ exp b 1 /C0 fp w=b /C18/C19 /C0 1 ð4Þ 2.2. Effect of heterogeneous nucleation The heterogeneous nucleation effect of FA contributes to the ce- ment hydration extent by surface absorption and precipitation. This effect is greatly inﬂuenced by the particle size or speciﬁc area of FA. Based on the experimental results, Lawrence et al. [8] ob- tained the following expression to account for the heterogeneous nucleation (HN) effect of FA, DaHN a0 ¼ w=b 1 þð hc =Seff Þht ð5Þ where DaHN is the hydration extent augmentation by HN effect, hc is a constant (8300 m 2/kg) and ht is assumed to be dependent on curing age. The term Seff is the effective surface area (m 2/kg), ex- pressed as a function of FA speciﬁc area Sp, FA mass fraction fp and weight function e [8]: Seff ¼ Sp fp 1 /C0 fp eðfpÞð 6Þ with the weight function e expressed as [8]: e ¼ 1 þ cosðfp /C1pÞ 2 /C20/C21 0:7 /C11 þ fp 36:8 /C18/C193:4"# /C0 1 ð7Þ The ht parameter is regressed in this article by an exponential func- tion in terms of curing age tc, ht ¼ 1 /C0 h1 exp /C0 h2 tc /C18/C19 ð8Þ with h1,2 calibrated as 0.612, 0.370 from data in [8]. 2.3. that very good agreement is obtained, thus this synthetic model is capable to describe the incremental effect of non-pozzolanic addi- tives on cement hydration extent. 3. Materials and experiments 3.1. Materials A Portland cement and a low calcium ﬂy-ash are retained for this study. The used cement is PI type according to Chinese standard [26], corresponding to CEM I in EN197 [27] and Type I in ASTM standard [28]. The chemical composition and physical properties of cement and ﬂy-ash are presented in Table 3. The mineral con- tents of cement were analyzed, through Bogue’s procedure [29],a sC 2S (21.38%), C3S (58.88%), C 3A (6.49%), C 4AF (8.77%), Gypsum (0.75%) and others (3.73%). The blended cement pastes in study adopt two water to binder ratios ( w/b) and four FA contents ( fp), the material proportioning given in Table 4 . 3.2. Test methods The TGA method was used to determine the amount of non-evaporable water (Wn) and calcium hydroxide (CH). Crushed samples from hardened pastes at given ages (7d, 28d, 90d, 180d) were ground to powder with maximum particle size con- trolled to 80 lm. Then the powder samples were oven-dried for 48 h at 50 ± 1 /C176C, a recommended procedure avoiding the hydrates decomposition [30]. Then the dried samples were subject to TGA test by a device of 2050/TA type. The Wn values were obtained from the weight loss of samples between 105 /C176C and 900 /C176C, illustrated in Fig. 3 , and calculated as [13,20], Wn ¼ m900 /C0 m105 m900 /C0ð fc /C1LOIc þ fp /C1LOIp Þð 11Þ where m105 and m900 are respectively the sample weights measured at 105 /C176C and 900 /C176C, fc,p the mass fractions of cement and FA in binder and LOI c,p the ignition losses of cement and FA. The subscripts c, p represent the cement and pozzolana without special mention in this article. The maximum temperature in TGA of this study was 900 /C176C, lower than the complete release temperature for non-evaporable water (1000 /C176C), but the weight loss higher than 850 /C176C was found to be insigniﬁcant [31]. The CH values were evaluated by sample weight loss during TGA at the decom- position temperature of CH (about 450 /C176C), see Fig. 3 b and d. The details of CH amount evaluation from TGA curves can be found in [31]. A selective dissolution procedure using picric acid and water was adopted to determine the pozzolanic reaction extent of ﬂy-ash, conforming to the guide of ACI Committee 211 [32]. The principle of selective dissolution is that the minerals in cement grain, cement hydrates and FA hydrates are all acid-soluble and only the unreacted part of ﬂy-ash particles is insoluble in picric acid [16,17]. In this study, this assumption was further corrected by a reference test on the residue measure- ment of picric dissolution on cement grains and FA particles. These values were used as the basis for hydration extent evaluation. Then the powder samples of blended pastes were subject to picric acid dissolution test and the pozzolanic reac- tion extent is evaluated by, ap ¼ 1 /C0 Rb /C0 m900 =m0 /C1ð1 þ fp /C1LOIp þ fc /C1LOIc Þ/C1fc Rc m900 =m0 /C1ð1 þ fp /C1LOIp þ fc /C1LOIc Þ/C1fp Rp ð12Þ This equation is adapted from the expression from Li et al. [20], taking into account further the ignition losses. In the equation, Rb,p,c represent the dissolution residue of sample, unreacted FA and cement respectively, and m0 is the initial sample weight. 4. Hydration extents of FA cement pastes 4.1. Once the non evaporable water Wc n; Wp n are determined, the cement hydration extent ac and FA reac- tion extent ap are, ac ¼ Wc n Wc n ð1Þ ; ap ¼ Wp n Wp n ð1Þ ð14Þ From the same reasoning, the hydration extents can also be ex- pressed in terms of CH content, ac ¼ CHc CHc ð1Þ ; ap ¼ CHp CHpð1Þ ð15Þ where CH c(1) = 0.204, CH p(1)= /C0 0.853 are estimated by stoichi- ometry analysis of cement and FA hydrations in Appendix A. Eqs. (13)–(15) provide actually an experimental evaluation approach for hydration extents ac,p, and this method is to be used later in the hydration extent quantiﬁcation. Using Eq. (10) and considering the proportionality in Eqs. (14) and (15) , Wc n and CH c can also be determined from the known W0 n; CH0 for neat paste with the same w/b ratio, Wc n W0 n ¼ CHc CH0 ¼ exp b 1 /C0 fp w=b /C18/C19 þ w=b 1 þð hc =Seff Þht ð16Þ The cement hydration extent ac can thus be determined. Further- more, as Wc n; CHc are known, the FA hydration extent ap can be eval- uated from Eq. (13b) as, ap ¼ Wt n /C0 Wc nfc Wp n ð1Þfp ¼ CHt /C0 CHc fc CHpð1Þfp ð17Þ Eqs. (16) and (17) provide another way to evaluate the hydration extents on the basis of the synthetic model. This model-based ap- proach is to be also used in quantifying the hydration extents. 4.2. Wn and CH results The measurement of Wn for all samples is illustrated in Fig. 4 , ﬁgured out by black ﬁlled circles. For neat pastes (PI0, PII0), W0 n in- creases with curing age. For PI0 ( w/b = 0.5), W0 n augments by 60% from 7d to 90d and by 70% for PII0 ( w/b = 0.3). For all ages the W0 n of PI0 is systematically higher than PII0 due to higher w/b con- tribution to cement hydration. For the blended pastes PI1-3 and PII1-3, the total non evaporable content, Wt n, increases with curing age for each FA content fp, and decreases with FA content. The dilu- tion effect of FA, taken as inert ﬁllers, on Wt n is illustrated by blue lines in Fig. 4 . The dash green lines take into account the effect of local w/c augmentation by FA replacement using the model in Fig. 5. Contribution of pure dilution effect (solid line), local w/c effect (dark gray area), heterogeneous nucleation (light gray area) and FA hydration (grid-ﬁlled area) to Wt n of PI samples at 28d. Eq. (4). Furthermore, the dash green lines consider the heteroge- neous nucleation effect by Eq. (5). A more detailed illustration is gi- ven in Fig. 5 for different contributions to Wt n. The difference between the dash green line and the measured Wt n can be attrib- uted to the FA hydration contribution Wp n. The CH content of all cement pastes is illustrated in black ﬁlled circles in Fig. 6 . It can be seen that the CH content of Paste I sam- ples is larger than that of Paste II samples due to larger hydration extent of cement. Different from the observation on Wt n values, the CH content does not show clear correlation with curing age due to the opposite contribution of cement hydration and FA hydration to CH content. The pure dilution effect of FA, local w/c effect and the heterogeneous nucleation effect on CH content are illustrated in Fig. 6 , respectively, by blue lines, red dash lines and green dash lines. The relative importance of these factors on CH content is pre- sented in details in Fig. 7 . The difference between the dash green line and measured CH contents is due to the FA hydration con- sumption CH p. 4.3. Synthetic effect of FA on blended cement hydration As previously described, the synthetic effects of FA in the hydra- tion process of blended cement pastes include the pure dilution ef- fect, local w/c augmentation effect as well as heterogeneous nucleation effect. Taking the pure dilution effect (diluted cement grains by inert ﬁllers) as the reference state, blue lines in Figs. 4 and 6 , the incremental amounts of non-evaporable water ( DWn) and CH content ( DCH), compared to the corresponding neat paste, can be splitted into three contributions, DWn ¼ð DWnÞw=c þð DWnÞHN þ Wp n ð18aÞ DCH ¼ð DCHÞw=c þð DCHÞHN þ CHp ð18bÞ The third terms at the right side of Eq. (18) represent the contribu- tion or consumption effect of FA reaction on Wn and CH contents, illustrated by the area between the red dash line and black circle line in Figs. 5 and 7 . As aforementioned, DWn is directly from mea- surement (DWn)w/c,HN are evaluated respectively from Eqs. (4), (14) and Eqs. (5), (14) , and Wp n is deduced from Eq. (18a). The values of DWn, ðDWnÞw=c;HN; Wp n are presented in Table 5. The four terms in Eq. (18a) show slight increment with curing age and the maximum va- lue of DWn is reached for FA content of 40%. Similar observations were also obtained in the literature [8,9]. The values of DCH are measured, the terms ( DCH)w/c,HN are evaluated respectively through Eqs. (4), (15) and Eqs. (5), (15) , and CH p is evaluated from Eq. (18b). These values are presented in Table 6 . The values of CH p are negative to denote the CH con- sumption. At the age of 7d, DCH values are slightly negative for Paste I samples but positive for Paste II samples, due to the more advanced FA reaction extent in Paste I samples. At the age of 90d, with the advancement of FA reaction the DCH values are all negative, meaning the FA pozzolanic reaction has substantially de- creased the CH content in hardened pastes. Fig. 7. 4.4. Cement hydration extent The experimental and model-based approaches, established in Section 4.1, are used to quantify the cement hydration extent ac. The experimental approach is to solve directly the equation array in Eqs. (13a), (13b) and obtain the hydration extent by Eq. (14) or Eq. (15). The ac solved by experimental approach is presented in Fig. 8 a and b. For both Paste I and II samples, the ac augments with curing age by hydration process. Not surprisingly, the cement hydration extent ac of Paste I samples is larger than the corre- sponding Paste II samples, reﬂecting clearly the effect of higher w/b ratio on cement hydration. For Paste I or II samples, the cement hydration extent ac increases systematically with ﬂy-ash content fp due to both the local w/c augmentation and heterogeneous nucle- ation effects. The model-based approach to determine ac consists in solving Eqs. (16) and (17) with W0 n; CH0 measured for neat pastes. The re- sults are presented in Fig. 9 in terms of the ac values from the experimental approach. It can be observed that the ac values from the two approaches are correlated to a linear relationship. This observation validates actually the synthetic model for local w/c effect and heterogeneous nucleation effect of FA in Eq. (10). 4.5. FA pozzolanic reaction extent The pozzolanic reaction extent of FA, ap, in blended pastes can also be evaluated in experimental and model-based approaches. The experimental approach consists in measuring the mass losses of ignition and mass residues by selective dissolution method and quantifying the reaction extent ap by Eq. (12). The obtained values of ap are presented in Fig. 10 . It can be observed that ap of Paste I samples is systematically higher than that of Paste II sam- ples. This is due to the fact that Paste I samples, w/b = 0.5, provide Fig. 8. Cement hydration extent solved by experimental approach for Paste I samples (a) and Paste II samples (b). Fig. 9. Cement hydration extent ac by experimental approach versus ac values from model-based approach. Fig. 10. Pozzolanic reaction extent ap determined by dissolution method for Paste I samples (a) and Paste II samples (b). 566 Q. Zeng et al. / Construction and Building Materials 27 (2012) 560–569

more pore solution (CH content) for the later FA pozzolanic reac- tion. The ap values decrease with FA content, e.g. ap at fp = 20% is almost double of ap at fp = 60%. The FA reaction extent evolves sub- stantially with curing age, i.e. ap = 0.05–0.10 at 7d and ap = 0.2–0.4 at 90d. Furthermore, the ap values are not sensitive to curing age within 28d for high w/b (Paste I) samples. The model-based approach is to determine Wp n or CH p through Eq. (18) and derive ap by Eq. (14) or Eq. (15). If only w/c effect is taken into account in Eq. (18a), the derived ap is presented in Fig. 11a in terms of measured pozzolanic reaction extent. An over- estimation of pozzolanic reaction extent is observed, and this is due to the fact that the physical contribution of FA to the cement hydration is underestimated. In other words, not only the higher w/c ratio can promote the cement hydration, but also the cement hydration can be accelerated by the heterogeneous nucleation ef- fect provided by FA particles. Fig. 11 b presents the ap values de- rived from Eq. (18b) in terms of measured values. Again, only the local w/c effect is considered in the prediction. Contrary to the Wn-based estimation in Fig. 11 (a), this CH-based estimation underestimates the FA reaction extent signiﬁcantly. The same rea- son for overestimation from Wn-based estimation is responsible for the underestimation by CH-based approach: the underestima- tion of the cement hydration degree in blended pastes induces also the underestimation of CH formation. Analogue to the single effect of w/c illustrated in Fig. 11, the sin- gle effect of heterogeneous nucleation of FA particles on cement hydration is presented in Fig. 12 a and b, respectively from Wn and CH values. Same observation is obtained for the predicted and measured FA reaction extent. Finally, Fig. 13 a and b present the estimated FA reaction extent, through Wn and CH values, in terms of measured values. Here both local w/c effect and heteroge- neous nucleation effect are considered in Eq. (18). Compared with Figs. 11 and 12 , much better agreement is found in Fig. 13a and b, validating again the synthetic model for cement hydration in blended pastes with both w/c and heterogeneous nucleation effects. 5. Conclusions 1, This article intends to quantify the hydration extents for cement grains and FA particles in blended cement pastes. To aid the interpretation of experimental data, a synthetic model is ﬁrstly established for cement hydration extent with the presence of FA. This model incorporates the pure dilu- tion effect, the local w/c augmentation effect and the heter- ogeneous nucleation effect of FA on cement hydration extent. This model is validated by literature data and used for cement hydration extent in blended pastes. Fig. 11. Comparison between the measured FA reaction extents and the predicted values from Wn-based estimation (a) and CH-based estimation (b) with only local w/c effect. Fig. 12. Comparison between the measured FA reaction extents and the predicted values from Wn-based estimation (a) and CH-based estimation (b) with only heterogeneous nucleation effect. Q.
